slides.title: The Grand Shift 🌍
slides.content:
Up until now, we've used primitive building blocks like `int` and `boolean`, stringing them together inside `main`.

But Java is an **Object-Oriented** language. In standard Java, almost everything is an Object. 

So what exactly is an Object?

slides.title: The Blueprint 🧬
slides.content:
Imagine you are building a neighborhood.

First, you hire an architect. They draw a **Blueprint** on paper. The blueprint dictates: "A house must have a color, a number of doors, and a method to open the garage."

In Java, this blueprint is called a **Class**.

slides.title: The House 🏠
slides.content:
You can't live inside a Blueprint. It's just a design.

You give the blueprint to builders, and they build a real, physical House out of wood and bricks.

In Java, that real, physical entity built from the Class is called an **Object** (or an Instance).

quizData.question: In the analogy of a car factory, what represents the Class and what represents the Object?

options.text: The Class is the physical Car you drive; the Object is the 3D Model on the computer.
The Class is the Factory building; the Object is the workers.
The Class is the design documentation; the Object is the physical Car rolling off the assembly line.

quizData.explanation: A Class defines WHAT the thing should look like and how it acts. An Object is the actual, usable thing created from that definition.

slides.title: Instantiation 🏗️
slides.content: The act of taking a Class blueprint and creating a real Object out of it is called **Instantiation**. We do this using the `new` keyword.

quizData.question: Which keyword acts as the 'Builder', taking a Class and physically allocating memory to create a new Object?
quizData.explanation: `new` tells the computer to fetch memory and establish a brand new instance based on the class design.
